A1.label: Positive divisors of 1 (exhaustive enumeration)
A1.method: Exhaustive enumeration of divisors d in [1, n] where n % d == 0

claim_formal.subject: the integer 1
claim_formal.property: whether 1 satisfies the definition of a prime number
claim_formal.operator_note: A prime number is defined as a natural number greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself. For 1 to be prime, it must satisfy BOTH conditions: (1) greater than 1, and (2) exactly two distinct positive divisors. We check whether 1 meets these criteria. The claim asserts 1 IS prime (== True); disproof requires showing it fails at least one definitional criterion.

claim_natural: The integer 1 is a prime number.

adversarial_checks.question: Was 1 ever historically considered a prime number?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Researched the history of primality of 1. Until the mid-19th century, many mathematicians (including Goldbach, Euler, and Lebesgue) considered 1 to be prime. The modern convention excluding 1 was formalized to preserve the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (unique prime factorization). The modern definition (ISO 80000-2, Hardy & Wright, etc.) explicitly requires primes to be > 1.
adversarial_checks.finding: Historically, 1 was sometimes considered prime, but the modern standard mathematical definition (universally adopted since ~1899) excludes 1. The claim is evaluated against the current standard definition.

adversarial_checks.question: Is there any modern mathematical authority that defines 1 as prime?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Checked ISO 80000-2 (international standard for mathematical notation), major textbooks (Hardy & Wright, Niven Zuckerman & Montgomery, Ireland & Rosen), and computational references (OEIS A000040). All define primes as integers greater than 1.
adversarial_checks.finding: No modern mathematical authority defines 1 as prime. The exclusion is universal in contemporary mathematics.

adversarial_checks.question: Does the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic break if 1 is prime?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: The FTA states every integer > 1 has a unique prime factorization (up to order). If 1 were prime, factorizations would not be unique: e.g., 6 = 2 × 3 = 1 × 2 × 3 = 1 × 1 × 2 × 3. This is the key mathematical reason 1 is excluded from primes.
adversarial_checks.finding: Including 1 as prime would destroy unique factorization, confirming that the modern exclusion is mathematically necessary, not arbitrary.
